video_title: Luis Laboy: Why Every Market Is Now an Emerging Market | Hewlett Foundation
gist: Luis Laboy, Director of Public Equities at the Hewlett Foundation, argues that geopolitical, policy, and market-structure risks once unique to emerging markets now describe every market, so allocators should rethink portfolio construction around quality and disruption risk. He details Hewlett's manager-selection machinery (explore/exploit sleeves, kill lists, need-vs-want criteria) and concrete positioning shifts (cutting value managers post-COVID, adding Japan).

themes.theme: Every Market Is an Emerging Market

insights.claim: Luis Laboy argues that geopolitical risk, political uncertainty, social polarization, policy risk, and changing market structure — traits once used to define emerging markets — now describe every market in the world, collapsing the old EM-vs-developed distinction.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy (guest, Hewlett Foundation Director of Public Equities)
insights.so_what: If the EM/DM dichotomy no longer holds, allocators should apply emerging-market-style political and structural risk analysis to developed-market portfolios and rethink asset-class buckets.
insights.quote: If I describe a market to you and I tell you this market has geopolitical risk, it has political uncertainty. It has social polarization. It's got policy risk.

insights.claim: Laboy contends the central task for allocators today is questioning whether they are asking the right questions about markets, because frameworks built over prior careers are now being invalidated by structural change.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A signal to interrogate inherited assumptions (currency, growth, factor) rather than plug them into models; edge comes from reframing questions, not refining spreadsheets.
insights.quote: are we asking the right questions about markets

insights.claim: Laboy invokes Anna Marshall's 'depends on why' answer — that whether a strong or weak currency helps emerging markets has no fixed answer — as evidence that the underlying question is often wrong and that models cannot substitute for causal understanding.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Warns quants and macro traders against seeking single-variable rules; the driver's sign depends on the underlying cause each cycle.
insights.quote: is a strong currency or a weak currency good or bad for emerging markets? And she just said, depends on why

insights.claim: During COVID, Hewlett reduced exposure to value managers on the view that value would be a tougher place going forward, and transitioned toward a quality, compounding-oriented equity portfolio.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A named endowment tilting from value toward quality compounders around 2020 is a concrete style-rotation datapoint for factor-aware LPs.
insights.quote: we ended up starting to uh reduce our exposure to for example value managers. We thought value would just be a tougher place to be going forward

insights.claim: Hewlett arrived at the Japan opportunity via technical analysis — spotting a breakout in the market — then did emerging-markets-style ground research before eventually building a Japan position.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Confirms an institutional LP is invested in Japan and shows technicals used as a top-down idea-generation trigger, not just risk timing.
insights.quote: we got to Japan idea through technical analysis through understanding a breakout in markets

insights.claim: Laboy frames technology and AI as an accelerating disruption risk that no longer separates 'tech' from the rest of the market — old-economy companies will increasingly differentiate based on how they apply technology across their stack.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Argues for evaluating every company (not just tech names) on AI/tech adoption; supports managers who assess the 'tech stack' of traditional businesses.
insights.quote: It's no longer about tech versus the rest of the world. It's old economy names are now going to differentiate themselves based on how they apply tech.

insights.claim: Hewlett structures its portfolio around a widening set of disruption risks — technology, regulatory/government intervention, policy, geopolitical, and value-chain risk — and selects managers who explicitly manage disruption risk in their process.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Maps the specific risk axes an endowment now underwrites; managers marketing 'disruption risk management' are being actively sought.
insights.quote: AI is apparently going to eat the world

insights.claim: Laboy applies the technical-analysis concept of 'curve fitting' — over-adapting to the past — to portfolio construction, deliberately letting go of biases and building the book dynamically rather than as a fixed asset-class checklist.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A caution against anchoring allocations to historical relationships; favor dynamic, forward-looking sizing over static policy weights.
insights.quote: the risk of over adapting to the past

insights.claim: Hewlett splits its equity book into a concentrated core portfolio (exploit) plus a carved-out next-generation manager sleeve (explore) with a lower confidence hurdle — not a lower quality hurdle — to keep a steady velocity of new ideas without diluting the core.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A reusable structure for concentrated allocators who otherwise can't onboard emerging managers because the incumbent bar is too high.
insights.quote: You're lowering the confidence hurdle. You're not lowering the hurdle of quality.

insights.claim: For every manager, Hewlett maintains a 'kill list' and 'key debates' — borrowed from Annie Duke's book 'Quit' — enumerating what would drive a redemption, so exits are made for fundamental reasons rather than chasing performance.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A pre-commitment device to counter the disposition effect in manager redemptions; concrete practice LPs can copy.
insights.quote: we've borrowed this from Annie Duke and her book quit, right, is we've put together what we call as a kill list

insights.claim: Laboy says most manager redemptions at Hewlett happen not because the manager is failing but because Hewlett is shifting its own strategy, leaving strong managers with no remaining alignment to the portfolio's needs.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Reframes redemptions as portfolio-fit decisions, not performance verdicts — relevant to GPs interpreting why an LP left.
insights.quote: you end up redeeming from a manager because we're shifting our strategy

insights.claim: Laboy insists on defining manager-selection criteria in advance because the biggest danger is picking the 'shiny object' you want rather than what the portfolio needs — otherwise you inject your own biases as a factor risk.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Pre-set criteria act as a bias-control mechanism; treats an allocator's personal preference as an unhedged factor exposure.
insights.quote: you look for what you want instead of what you need

insights.claim: The single most important thing Hewlett looks for in a manager is coherence — alignment between the manager's investment strategy and how they actually think and their emotional makeup — not adherence to a borrowed model.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A due-diligence lens: probe whether a manager's process is internalized or imitated (e.g. copying Buffett), as imitation signals fragility.
insights.quote: their investment strategy aligns with how they think, right? Their emotional makeup. That's the most important thing we look for with a manager

insights.claim: Laboy separates opportunity-set conviction from manager selection: Hewlett first gains conviction on the opportunity set, then decides which asset class best expresses it, then the right strategy, then finally the manager.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A top-down-then-bottom-up sequencing that GPs can anticipate; managers are selected last, as executors of a pre-formed thesis.
insights.quote: we gain conviction on the opportunity set

insights.claim: Laboy warns that when a manager uses a buzzword like 'quality compounders,' they cede control of the conversation to the LP, who fills the gap with their own interpretation — and any later process detail that contradicts it can sink the relationship in a single meeting.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Actionable for GPs raising capital: define terms like 'quality' precisely because LPs have limited time and won't grant a second meeting after a perceived contradiction.
insights.quote: the minute a manager comes in and tells you that they invest in quality compounders... they've seated control of the conversation now to the listener

insights.claim: Laboy states 'quality' is a spectrum, not a fixed point, making it one of the most dangerous words in allocator conversations because speaker and listener rarely mean the same thing.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Forces precision in style definitions; LPs should ask managers to operationalize 'quality' rather than accept the label.
insights.quote: quality is a spectrum, right? It's not a fixed point

insights.claim: Laboy credits 15 years marketing a hedge fund with letting him interpret manager answers from the inside — he once cut off a manager's evasive risk-management answer by noting he 'used to give your exact answer,' and the manager conceded they lacked a strong risk practice.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Illustrates a diligence tactic: recognizing rehearsed non-answers by pattern-matching to one's own former pitches.
insights.quote: I just spent 15 years marketing and I used to give your exact answer. What I'm picking up here is that you guys really don't have a very strong risk management practice

insights.claim: Laboy notes the decision cadence differs sharply between direct investing (high turnover, lighter analysis per decision) and LP allocation (far fewer decisions, much deeper analysis), and mis-calibrating it leads to either uninformed decisions or analysis paralysis.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Relevant for investors transitioning between direct and allocator seats; the analytical dial must be deliberately re-tuned.

insights.claim: Hewlett's public equity team looks through its managers to the underlying holdings, building spreadsheets of all underlying positions and treating the book as a single portfolio of companies while concentrating a formerly large manager roster.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Signals an LP that monitors aggregate factor and name concentration across managers — GPs may see look-through overlap questions.
insights.quote: we started to you know I started to build out spreadsheets that showed all the underlying positions

insights.claim: Hewlett's small public-equity team (Laboy and Anna Marshall as CIO, plus directors Brett Johnson and Tom Mitchkowski) surfaces half-baked manager ideas at Monday meetings so that no final decision surprises anyone on the team.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Names Hewlett's decision-makers and shows a consensus-building cadence GPs can map when navigating the relationship.

insights.claim: Laboy describes a permanent tension between spending time on existing roster managers versus sourcing new ones, arguing getting current holdings right is as important as finding the next manager and requires an increasing share of time.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A time-allocation model for allocators; monitoring incumbents deserves parity with prospecting, not residual attention.

insights.claim: Laboy's formative investing lesson from Everest's Marco was that the job is to make money, not to be right — a distinction he now applies differently to core versus emerging-manager sleeves.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A mental model separating ego from P&L; also shapes how much conviction is required before sizing a position.
insights.quote: You're trying to be right rather than make money. And our job is to make money.

insights.claim: Laboy recounts a contrarian early-2000s Brazil trade: after meeting Lula's chief of staff and central bank when the market expected devaluation and default, Everest maxed its Brazil limit and added an options structure needing a 20% rally in two months for a 5:1 payout — the market rose 23% and the trade paid off.
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: A case study in high-conviction contrarian macro sizing against consensus default fears; illustrates convex option expression of a directional macro view.
insights.quote: within two months, the market up went up 23%. We made our 5 to1 payout on our option strategy

insights.claim: Laboy's advice to young allocators is to model every investor you meet but never adopt anyone's process wholesale — an edge is personal and must align with how you think, captured as 'borrow freely from others but make it your own.'
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Warns against cargo-culting famous investors (e.g. Buffett); a coherent, internalized process is the durable edge.
insights.quote: borrow freely and liberally from others, but in the end, make it your own

insights.claim: Laboy learned from Anna Marshall to always add distinct value to a team as insurance against inevitable bad performance years — he used heavy marketing at Everest as both defense (a buffer) and offense (working through the thesis in meetings).
insights.speaker: Luis Laboy
insights.so_what: Career-durability framework for investment professionals: build a second axis of contribution beyond raw returns.
